This trend is driven by a desire for consistency and reliability on the field, as well as concerns about animal welfare. articles.title: How Liminalism Became the Defining Aesthetic of Our Time
articles.summary: Giorgio de Chirico's "The Red Tower" (1913) has become a defining aesthetic of our time, evoking feelings of disquiet and nostalgia in those who experience it. This eerie image, captured by Dave Columbus on Facebook, exemplifies the popular internet aesthetic of liminality, where past, present, and future collide. The abandoned Century III Mall in West Mifflin, Pennsylvania is a prime example of this phenomenon, as its empty spaces and vacant stores create a sense of melancholic discomfort that resonates with contemporary artists and internet users alike.

articles.title: Tokenomics: Quantifying Where Tokens Are Used in Agentic Software Engineering
articles.summary: Tokenomics, a field that studies the quantification of tokens in software development, has been applied to analyze the operational efficiency and resource consumption of Large Language Models (LLMs) in automated software engineering tasks. The study examines token distribution across distinct stages of the Software Development Life Cycle, revealing that the iterative Code Review stage accounts for the majority of token consumption, with input tokens constituting the largest share. This finding suggests that the primary cost of agentic software engineering lies not in initial code generation but in automated refinement and verification.

articles.title: How LLMs Actually Work
articles.summary: Modern language models like transformers rely on a complex set of algorithms and techniques, including tokenization, embeddings, positional encoding, attention, the feed-forward network, residual streams, and layer normalization. These components work together to predict the next token in a sequence, allowing the model to generate coherent text. The architecture of these models is shared across many different variants, but their training data and configuration choices can significantly impact their performance.

articles.title: Ntsc-rs – open-source video emulation of analog TV and VHS artifacts
articles.summary: Ntsc-rs is an open-source video effect that accurately emulates analog TV and VHS artifacts by using algorithms to model the actual transmission and encoding processes of these formats. Unlike similar effects such as ntscQT, it can run in real time at much higher resolutions than actual NTSC footage. Developed in Rust and multithreaded for SIMD acceleration, ntsc-rs is available as a standalone application, web app, or plugin for various video editing software.

Developers who write C code should take note of this finding to ensure their applications are secure.

articles.title: A Little Explanation of Little's Law
articles.summary: A simple idea from computer science can be applied to many real-world scenarios where queues are involved. Little's Law states that if you have enough resources (such as coffee machines or employees), the average time customers spend waiting for service is directly proportional to the rate at which they arrive and inversely proportional to the length of the line. This means that increasing the throughput by adding more resources can help reduce wait times, making it a useful concept in many fields beyond just coffee shops.

articles.title: Zeroserve: A zero-config web server you can script with eBPF
articles.summary: Zeroserve is a zero-config web server that can be scripted with eBPF, offering fast performance and efficient scripting capabilities. It allows developers to create a declarative configuration language for routing, headers, authentication, rate limiting, and proxying, while also providing an optional scripting runtime bolted on the side. This enables a single, ordinary program to see every request and decide what happens, resulting in a compact and deployable solution with no config file required.

articles.title: Ergonomic overrides for Nixpkgs

This project aims to address the fundamental problem of type-checking Nix, where nobody actually uses it at any significant scale, by creating embedded languages within Nix for programming "in the large". By designing better abstractions, such as override functions and overlays, developers can build more user-friendly tools that work well in inexpert hands.

articles.title: Moving beyond fork() + exec()

Li Chen's proposal for spawn templates aims to optimize this pattern by creating a template for an executable file that can be reused across multiple invocations, reducing the overhead of copying and discarding memory.

articles.title: The perils of UUID primary keys in SQLite
articles.summary: Random UUIDs can cause significant extra paging in clustered indexes, leading to poor performance. This is because the random nature of UUIDs means that rows are inserted into the B-tree in a seemingly random order, requiring re-balancing which can be time-consuming. The use of random UUIDs as primary keys can lead to wasted space and increased latency in databases like SQLite.

articles.title: Total Reciprocity Public License
articles.summary: The Total Reciprocity Public License is an open-source software license that allows for free use, modification, and distribution of software without any restrictions. It was created in 2004 by Mark Shuttleworth as a response to the GNU General Public License, which restricts the ability to modify and distribute software. The TRL license has been widely adopted by developers due to its permissive terms, making it easier for companies to integrate open-source components into their products.

articles.title: The smallest C++ binary
articles.summary: The smallest C++ binary is a program that runs successfully with no errors and produces a deterministically zero exit status. It must be created using the GCC compiler, stripped of debugging information, and produced by only linking against the standard library. The resulting binary can be as small as 13632 bytes, but it still includes some code that is not executed before entering the main function.

articles.title: Stop Using Conventional Commits
articles.summary: Conventional Commits promises to add semantic meaning to commit messages but fails to deliver on its promise by prioritizing type over scope, which is the most important part of a commit. The format typically used for Conventional Commits, where type comes first and then optional scope and description, has a major failing: it puts type ahead of scope. This means that contributors and other stakeholders who care about the scope of changes are often ignored in favor of the type of change. As a result, Conventional Commits fails to provide meaningful information for debugging, incident response, or understanding changes made in a commit.
